monday.description: Build clean coordination between your fretting and picking hands. Speed comes from accuracy and relaxation, not force.

exercises.name: Ascending Chromatic Motif
exercises.tips: Keep both hands as relaxed as possible and monitor your forearms for unnecessary tension. Use the minimum amount of finger pressure required to sound each note cleanly. If tension appears, slow the tempo and rebuild accuracy before increasing speed.

exercises.name: Asymmetrical Chromatic Grouping
exercises.tips: Maintain strict alternate picking even as the accents shift against the beat. Do not allow the accent pattern to alter your picking mechanics. Focus on rhythmic consistency rather than speed.

exercises.name: Burst Then Relax Run
exercises.description:
Alternate slow notes with fast bursts.

```
e|----------------5-6-7-8-|
B|------------------------|
   s s s s f f f f
```
exercises.tips: Treat the fast notes as short, controlled bursts rather than sustained speed. Actively release tension immediately after each burst. The goal is to teach your hands how fast playing should feel without fatigue.

exercises.name: Single-String Speed Cycling
exercises.description:
1-2-3-4 pattern on a single string.

```
e|-5-6-7-8-5-6-7-8-|
```
exercises.tips: Focus on even pick attack and consistent timing between notes. Because string crossings are removed, inefficiencies in your picking motion will be more obvious. Keep movements small and controlled.

exercises.name: Jazz: Bebop Chromatic Descent
exercises.description:
Smooth descending bebop line with chromatic passing tones.

```
e|-10-9-8-7-6-5-4-5-|
```
exercises.tips: Keep the chromatic tones passing and unaccented — imagine a saxophone phrasing this line. The resolution back up to fret 5 gives the phrase its bebop character. Aim for a legato, fluid quality rather than mechanical evenness.

exercises.name: Metal: Pedal Tone Scalar Burst
exercises.description:
Pedal tone alternating with ascending scale degrees.

```
e|-5-8-5-9-5-10-5-12-|
```
exercises.tips: The pedal (fret 5) should sound crisp and aggressive. Keep the picking motion tight — this is a precision drill common in thrash and progressive metal. Palm muting the pedal note adds a heavy, percussive quality.

exercises.name: Blues: Minor Pentatonic Box Descent
exercises.description:
Classic 3-string blues box descent.

```
e|-8-5------|
B|-----8-5--|
G|--------7-|
```
exercises.tips: Aim for the fluid, vocal quality of BB King and SRV. Small, subtle variations in pick attack define blues phrasing — avoid mechanical evenness. Every note should feel like it has intention and expression.

exercises.name: Classical: Ascending Thirds Sequence
exercises.description:
Diatonic third intervals ascending in sequence — Czerny inspired.

```
e|-5-8-7-10-8-12-10-13-|
```
exercises.tips: Every note must be identical in volume, timing, and tone — this is a classical finger equality drill. There is no room for accent irregularity. Keep your hand position neutral and fingers curved throughout the pattern.

tuesday.description: Improve accuracy and control when moving across non-adjacent strings.

exercises.name: Skipped Triad Shapes
exercises.tips: Pick deliberately and avoid brushing adjacent strings. Ensure unused strings are muted at all times. Accuracy and clean silence are more important than tempo.

exercises.name: Inside–Outside Skip Alternation
exercises.tips: Be consciously aware of whether each string change is inside or outside picking. Do not compensate with sweeping or economy picking. Keep the pick path intentional and consistent.

exercises.name: Muted Skip Accuracy Drill
exercises.description:
Muted fretting hand, focus on pick travel.

```
e|--x-----x-----x--|
B|------x-----x----|
G|x-----x----------|
```
exercises.tips: Lightly mute the strings with your fretting hand so pitch is removed from the exercise. Listen closely for missed strings or unintended contact. This drill isolates picking accuracy without distraction.

exercises.name: Single-Note Skip Ladder
exercises.tips: Move the pick deliberately between strings rather than relying on momentum. Prepare the next string crossing mentally before executing it. Keep the motion calm and controlled.

exercises.name: Jazz: Quartal String Skipping
exercises.description:
Perfect fourth intervals on alternating strings, skipping B string.

```
e|-5---7---9---|
B|-------------|
G|-9---11--13--|
```
exercises.tips: Quartal harmony (stacked 4ths) is a defining sound of modern jazz — McCoy Tyner, Coltrane. Focus on clean silence on the skipped B string. The angular interval leaps should feel deliberate and angular, not rushed.

exercises.name: Metal: String-Skip Power Arpeggio
exercises.description:
Minor arpeggio ascending across skipped strings (prog metal style).

```
e|--------5-|
B|------5---|
G|----5-----|
D|-7--------|
```
exercises.tips: Let each note ring slightly as you move through the arpeggio — the skip creates an expansive, atmospheric quality common in progressive and power metal. Keep the pick angle consistent across all string transitions.

exercises.name: Blues: Sliding Octave Shapes
exercises.tips: Mute all strings between A and B with your fretting hand — the shapes should ring as clean, warm octave pairs. Keep the wrist loose and let the shapes slide smoothly. This octave technique defines the warm, round sound of jazz and blues lead playing.

exercises.name: Rock: Wide Register Skip
exercises.description:
Jump between low and high register via string skip — classic rock lead style.

```
e|-5-7---------12-14-|
G|-------5-7-9-------|
```
exercises.tips: Visualize the target string before your pick moves. Wide string skips require a larger arc — let your wrist guide the movement naturally. This kind of wide-register skip is a staple of classic and hard rock lead lines.

wednesday.description: Develop strict alternate picking with challenging crossings.

exercises.name: Scalar Alternate Line
exercises.tips: Maintain down-up consistency at all times, even when it feels inefficient. Avoid the temptation to use economy picking on string changes. Clarity and rhythmic precision are the primary goals.

exercises.name: String-Crossing Displacement Run
exercises.tips: Allow mistakes to reveal weaknesses rather than correcting them with technique shortcuts. Keep the picking hand motion consistent across all groupings. Slow down if necessary to preserve form.

exercises.name: Two-String Crossing Loop
exercises.description:
Short loop across two strings.

```
e|--5-7-5-7-|
B|--5-7-5-7-|
```
exercises.tips: Focus on how the pick escapes the string after each stroke. Keep the loop smooth and even, without accenting string changes unintentionally. Small motions are critical here.

exercises.name: Accent Displacement Alternate Line
exercises.tips: Separate rhythmic accents from picking mechanics. The pick should move identically on accented and unaccented notes. Accents come from controlled emphasis, not extra force.

exercises.name: Jazz: Chromatic Enclosure
exercises.description:
Approach each target note from a half-step above and below — bebop phrasing.

```
e|-6-4-5-9-7-8-13-11-12-|
```
exercises.tips: Each 3-note group is: one fret above the target, one fret below, then the target. This is a fundamental bebop device. Practice slowly until the chromatic tones resolve naturally — the target note should feel 'arrived at', not rushed through.

exercises.name: Metal: Downpicking Endurance
exercises.description:
Strict downpicking only — no alternate picking allowed.

```
e|-7-7-7-7-7-7-7-7-|
```
exercises.tips: Downpicking is the defining technique of Metallica and Black Sabbath rhythm tones. Keep your wrist loose despite the fatigue — if your forearm tightens, slow down. The heavier, more controlled tone from pure downstrokes is the reward for the discipline.

exercises.name: Blues: Chromatic Turnaround Line
exercises.description:
Chromatic descent — the foundational sound of blues turnarounds.

```
e|-8-7-6-5-4-|
```
exercises.tips: This chromatic descending line from C to Ab is the DNA of blues turnarounds and jazz approach tones. Play it with a slight swing feel — dotted-note subdivision rather than even 16ths. Listen to BB King and Clapton for reference.

exercises.name: Classical: Alberti Bass Pattern
exercises.description:
Low-middle-high-middle broken chord texture — Beethoven / Mozart style.

```
e|-----8-----|
B|---5---5---|
G|-5---------|
```
exercises.tips: In Alberti bass, the middle voice (B string) should be slightly softer than the bass and melody. This builds right-hand independence and precise string targeting. Maintain strict alternate picking and let each voice have its own character.

thursday.description: Improve reach and independence. Comfort always comes first.

exercises.name: Four-Fret Expansion
exercises.tips: Stop immediately if you feel pain or sharp discomfort. Keep the thumb relaxed behind the neck to reduce strain. This exercise should feel challenging but controlled.

exercises.name: Held-Anchor Stretch Line
exercises.tips: Ensure the anchored finger remains planted throughout the entire pattern. Move the other fingers slowly and deliberately. Avoid twisting the wrist to reach notes.

exercises.name: Spider Stretch (1-3-2-4)
exercises.description:
Non-linear finger order.

```
e|--5-7-6-8-|
```
exercises.tips: Maintain a neutral wrist angle and curved fingers. Focus on finger independence rather than speed. This drill should feel mentally engaging but physically light.

exercises.name: Micro-Shift Stretch
exercises.description:
Shift up one fret each cycle.

```
e|--5---8--6---9--7---10-|
```
exercises.tips: Treat each shift as a fresh position rather than a continuation of the stretch. Keep movements slow and intentional. Do not rush through uncomfortable ranges.

exercises.name: Jazz: Wide Diagonal Voicing Reach
exercises.description:
Diagonal stretch reaching for wide chord voicing positions — jazz drop-voicing style.

```
e|------9-|
B|----8---|
G|--7-----|
D|-5------|
```
exercises.tips: Stop immediately if you feel pain. This diagonal shape represents the kind of wide-interval reaches required by jazz drop-2 and drop-3 chord voicings. Keep the thumb low and relaxed behind the neck. Move one finger at a time.

exercises.name: Metal: Power Chord Reach Shift
exercises.description:
Stretch between power chord positions — rhythm metal accuracy.

```
D|--5---9-|
A|--3---7-|
```
exercises.tips: Stop if you feel pain. Power chord shapes require the same stretch width regardless of tempo — this exercise trains the reach across positions. Keep the unused strings muted. Move precisely, not quickly.

exercises.name: Blues: Pre-Bend Stretch Preparation
exercises.description:
Wide finger spread to condition for blues string bending — BB King / SRV style.

```
e|-5---9-|
B|-5---8-|
```
exercises.tips: Stop if you feel any sharp pain. This stretch conditions the hand for the wide finger spans needed when bending blues strings. Keep the wrist low and the knuckles arched. Approach this one slowly.

exercises.name: Classical: Held Bass Independence Stretch
exercises.description:
Hold bass note while fingers move on upper strings — Segovia / Tárrega style.

```
e|------7-8-10-|
B|------8------|
G|------7------|
D|-10----------|
```
exercises.tips: Stop if you feel pain. Hold D string fret 10 firmly throughout while the other fingers move. This is a demanding classical independence exercise — the bass note must not waver. Move slowly and deliberately.

exercises.name: Hammer-On / Pull-Off Ladder
exercises.description:
Minimal picking.

```
e|--5h7p5--|
B|--5h7----|
```
exercises.tips: Aim for equal volume between picked and legato notes. Use finger strength rather than extra pick attack. Keep unused fingers relaxed and close to the fretboard.

exercises.name: Offset Legato Accents
exercises.description:
Accents shift.

```
e|--5h7h8p7p5--|
```
exercises.tips: Generate accents using finger pressure and timing, not picking. Maintain smooth legato motion even as accents move. Avoid tightening the hand to emphasize notes.

exercises.name: Descending Pull-Off Drill
exercises.description:
Descending pull-offs only.

```
e|--8p7p5--|
```
exercises.tips: Pull off with intention so each note rings clearly. Do not rely on gravity or finger collapse. This drill exposes and strengthens weaker fingers.

exercises.name: Held-Note Legato Independence
exercises.description:
One finger holds while others move.

```
e|--5h7h8--|
B|--5------|
```
exercises.tips: Keep the held note completely stable while other fingers move. Avoid sympathetic finger motion. This drill builds true finger independence and control.

exercises.name: Jazz: Bebop Enclosure Legato
exercises.description:
Approach each target note from above and below with hammer-ons and pull-offs — bebop technique.

```
e|-6p4h5-9p7h8-13p11h12-|
```
exercises.tips: Each 3-note group encircles the target: one fret above (pull-off), one fret below (hammer-on), target. This is one of the most important bebop phrasing devices. The legato touch makes the chromatic approach notes feel smooth and inevitable.

exercises.name: Metal: Tapped Hammer Pull-Off
exercises.description:
Right-hand tap into legato pull-off — Van Halen / Petrucci style.

```
e|-12t-5h7p5-|
    T  h  p
```
exercises.tips: Tap fret 12 firmly with your pick-hand index or middle finger, then pull off to reveal fret 5 (held by left index), hammer on to fret 7, pull off back to 5. Keep the tapped note aggressive — it should sound as loud as the legato notes.

exercises.name: Blues: Bend-Release Legato
exercises.description:
Full-step bend resolving into a legato descending line — SRV / Bonamassa style.

```
e|-8b10-8-7p5-|
```
exercises.tips: Bend fret 8 up a full step to the sound of fret 10, then release and continue descending with pull-offs. The bend must be in tune before you release. This is the core phrasing motion of blues lead guitar.

exercises.name: Classical: Ascending-Descending Slur Étude
exercises.description:
Hammer-ons ascending, pull-offs descending — Carcassi / Tárrega style.

```
e|-5h7h8h10p8p7p5-|
```
exercises.tips: Every slurred note must ring with the same volume as the picked notes. In classical technique, this is called tonal equality in legato. Keep the hand position stable and avoid collapsing the knuckles under the effort.

saturday.description: Train clean landings and confident shifts.

exercises.name: Position Jump Motifs
exercises.tips: Visualize the target fret before moving your hand. Land directly on the note without sliding. Accuracy should feel decisive, not tentative.

exercises.name: Blind Position Targeting
exercises.description:
Look away after first position.

```
e|--5-----------12--|
```
exercises.tips: Trust spatial awareness rather than visual confirmation. Move confidently and commit to the landing. Avoid micro-adjustments after contact.

exercises.name: Octave Jump Targeting
exercises.tips: Mentally map the octave shape before jumping. Keep the hand relaxed during the shift. Clean landings matter more than speed.

exercises.name: Delayed Landing Drill
exercises.description:
Pause before landing.

```
e|--5----(pause)----12--|
```
exercises.tips: Use the pause to clearly visualize the target position. Move only after you are confident in the landing. This trains intention rather than reaction.

exercises.name: Jazz: Guide Tone Voice-Leading
exercises.description:
Target the 3rd and 7th of each chord change — the guide tones of a ii-V-I in C.

```
e|-8-7---7-0-|
```
exercises.tips: Land precisely on each guide tone: C (7th of Dm7), B (3rd of G7), E (3rd of Cmaj7). The accuracy of these landings is what makes jazz lines sound harmonically connected. Hear the chord changing under you as you move.

exercises.name: Metal: Multi-Position Speed Accuracy
exercises.description:
Fast scalar run across three positions — Marty Friedman / Petrucci style.

```
e|-5-7-9--12-14-16-|
```
exercises.tips: Each position shift must be clean and decisive — no sliding allowed. The notes should sound even in volume despite the position changes. Build speed only after every shift lands cleanly at a slow tempo.

exercises.name: Blues: Vocal Phrasing Accuracy
exercises.description:
Call-and-response melodic phrasing — SRV / Buddy Guy style.

```
e|-10--8-7-5--|
B|-----------8|
```
exercises.tips: The descending line on e string is the 'call'. The landing on B string fret 8 is the 'response'. Land on the response note cleanly and let it breathe. Blues phrasing is about space as much as notes.

exercises.name: Classical: Ascending Position Étude
exercises.description:
Sequential position shifts ascending through three hand positions — Segovia scale style.

```
e|-5-7-8--8-10-12--12-13-15-|
```
exercises.tips: Each position group (5-7-8, 8-10-12, 12-13-15) must sound even before moving to the next. The shift between groups is the moment of risk — prepare it by visualizing the new position while still in the old one.

sunday.description: Smooth chord changes and continuous motion.

exercises.name: Triad Flow
exercises.tips: Lift fingers only as much as necessary between shapes. Allow transitions to overlap slightly for smoothness. Avoid stopping the sound between chords.

exercises.name: Voice-Led Triad Transitions
exercises.tips: Focus your attention on the moving voice rather than the full chord shape. Keep stationary notes firmly in place. Smoothness is more important than tempo.

exercises.name: Single-Finger Pivot Changes
exercises.tips: Use the anchored finger as a physical reference point. Let the other fingers move efficiently around it. Avoid lifting the pivot finger unnecessarily.

exercises.name: Rhythmic Delay Transitions
exercises.description:
Delayed changes.

```
e|--5----(wait)----7--|
```
exercises.tips: Stay relaxed even as the change is delayed. Keep the rhythm steady and controlled. This drill builds confidence during imperfect transitions.

exercises.name: Jazz: ii-V-I Voice Leading
exercises.description:
Smooth guide-tone voice leading through a jazz ii-V-I chord progression.

```
e|--5---3---5--|
B|--6---5---5--|
G|--4---4---4--|
```
exercises.tips: Focus on the smoothness of each voice moving (or not moving) between chords. One voice moves a half-step while others hold — this is the principle behind all great jazz voice leading. Keep the hand relaxed and transitions seamless.

exercises.name: Metal: Power Chord Riff Switching
exercises.description:
Fast power chord transitions between three positions — Metallica / Sabbath style.

```
D|--5-5-5--7-7-7--3-3-3-|
A|--3-3-3--5-5-5--1-1-1-|
```
exercises.tips: Each chord position must lock in immediately — no hesitation on the switch. Keep the right hand constant: the rhythm does not pause for the left hand. This is the core discipline of rhythm metal playing.

exercises.name: Blues: Dominant Chord Movement
exercises.description:
Chromatic dominant chord slide between positions — classic Chicago blues style.

```
e|--5-5-5--6-6-6--5-5-5-|
B|--5-5-5--6-6-6--5-5-5-|
G|--5-5-5--6-6-6--5-5-5-|
```
exercises.tips: This chromatic one-fret slide between chord positions is a core blues comping device — heard in Robert Johnson, Muddy Waters, and beyond. Keep the transitions locked in time. The one-fret move should feel inevitable, not rushed.

exercises.name: Rock: Sus2 to Major Resolution
exercises.description:
Suspended chord resolving to major — Hendrix / classic rock tension-and-release.

```
e|--5---5--|
B|--5---7--|
G|--7---7--|
```
exercises.tips: The suspended chord (Dsus2) creates tension — the resolution to D major is the release. Let the suspended chord breathe for a full beat before resolving. This tension-release motion is the emotional core of rock harmony.
